statement.markdown: You are given an array of integers and you need to left rotate the array by one position. For example, if the input array is [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], the output should be [2, 3, 4, 5, 1]. The first element of the array should be moved to the end of the array.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer N, the number of elements in the array. The second line contains N space-separated integers, the elements of the array.
statement.outputFormat: The output should be a single line containing N space-separated integers, the left rotated array.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array

examples.explanation: The first element 1 is moved to the end of the array
The first element 10 is moved to the end of the array

hints.text: you can solve this problem by shifting the first element of the array to the end. think about how you can do this in a single line of code.
you can use array slicing to achieve this. start by slicing the array from the second element to the end, then concatenate the result with the first element of the array.
consider using the modulo operator to handle cases where the array has only one element.
you can use array concatenation to move the first element to the end. think about how you can use the modulo operator to handle the rotation.
think about using the modulo operator to handle cases where the array has only one element, and then use array concatenation to move the first element to the end.

editorial.title: Left Rotate Array by One
editorial.intuition: The problem requires shifting the elements of the array one position to the left, which can be achieved by slicing the array and concatenating the parts in reverse order.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve using a temporary array to store the rotated elements, which would also result in a time complexity of O(n) but is less efficient due to the extra operations involved.
editorial.betterSolution: A more efficient solution is to use Python's list slicing feature to directly rotate the array, eliminating the need for a temporary array.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use list slicing to rotate the array in a single line of code, which not only achieves the desired result but also does so in a concise and readable manner.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proven by analyzing the slicing operation, which effectively shifts all elements one position to the left and wraps the first element around to the end of the array.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the solution with a sample input, such as [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], would produce the output [2, 3, 4, 5, 1], demonstrating the correctness of the solution.
commonMistakes: Using a temporary array to store the rotated elements
Implementing a manual loop to shift the elements
Failing to handle edge cases, such as an empty array
Incorrectly slicing the array, resulting in incorrect rotation
